questions.scenario: The orders team needs a new production invoice bucket. Its root module must create it, apply ownership tags, enable related controls, and remove it only through a reviewed retirement change. Which Terraform design expresses that lifecycle responsibility?

options.text: Declare the bucket as a managed resource in the orders production root module.
Read the bucket through a data source before it exists, then treat the lookup as lifecycle ownership.
Copy the planned bucket name into locals and let an external script create it after apply.
Publish the name as an output without declaring which configuration owns the real bucket.

questions.explanation: A resource block gives this root module lifecycle ownership: create, update, replace, and destroy actions can appear in its plans and state. A lookup reads existing provider data but does not create the object. Locals and outputs move values inside Terraform without assigning lifecycle ownership. See [Terraform resources](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/resources).

questions.scenario: The platform root module already owns `dp-platform-shared-logs-prod`. The orders root module needs its ARN for an IAM policy, but must not change the bucket's tags, retention, or deletion lifecycle. Which boundary is clearest?

options.text: Declare the shared bucket as a second resource in the orders root so both teams can converge it.
Read the platform-owned bucket through a lookup or published output, then manage only the orders-owned IAM policy.
Import the bucket into both states so each root can manage the attributes it cares about.
Hardcode the ARN in every policy and stop checking which stack owns the bucket.

questions.explanation: Ownership should remain with one stack. The consumer can read a provider lookup or an explicit published value and manage its own dependent policy. Declaring or importing the same object into two states creates competing ownership, while copied ARNs weaken traceability and drift handling.

questions.scenario: A provider reads the current AWS account ID for naming and tags. A reviewer says the lookup should be a resource because the AWS account is important infrastructure. What is the best response?

options.text: Use a resource because operational importance determines whether Terraform owns an object.
Import the account into state before any resource can reference the account ID.
Keep it read-only because this root needs account context but does not own the AWS account lifecycle.
Replace the lookup with a variable because provider-returned context cannot participate in references.

questions.explanation: The deciding question is lifecycle ownership, not importance. The configuration reads account context supplied by the provider and does not create or retire the AWS account. A variable could carry copied context, but it would not make the account managed or improve provider evidence.

questions.scenario: The orders root module manages its IAM role, reads a platform-owned VPC, and creates a security group in that VPC. Which two design comments preserve clear ownership and dependency information?

options.text: Keep the IAM role and security group as managed resources because this root owns their lifecycle.
Declare the VPC as a resource too so a reference can create an implicit dependency.
Reference the VPC lookup result from the security group instead of copying the VPC ID into another literal.
Duplicate the VPC ID in the resource and output so reviewers can compare two independent values.

questions.explanation: The root should manage objects it owns and read the shared VPC without claiming its lifecycle. Referencing the lookup result passes the provider ID and records the data flow. Redeclaring the VPC creates ownership conflict, while duplicate literals can drift and do not improve the graph.

questions.scenario:
A pull request changes a shared DNS zone from a lookup to this block:

```hcl
resource "aws_route53_zone" "platform" {
  name = "devpolaris.example"
}
```

The plan says `1 to add`. The platform team already owns the real zone. What is the strongest review conclusion?

options.text: The matching zone name makes the resource an automatic read-only reference to the existing object.
The create is harmless because a resource cannot affect objects that another Terraform state manages.
The plan proves the existing zone was absent from AWS when Terraform refreshed state.
The orders root is now proposing lifecycle ownership and creation, so the change conflicts with the stated shared-resource boundary.

questions.explanation: The block and create action mean this root intends to create and track a zone. Matching a real name does not turn a resource into a lookup or safely adopt the existing zone. The plan alone does not prove the provider account contains no zone, and duplicate ownership remains unsafe.

questions.scenario: A refactor renames `aws_s3_bucket.orders_exports` to `aws_s3_bucket.exports`. The configured bucket name is unchanged, but the plan proposes one destroy and one create. Which change best preserves the existing ownership binding?

options.text: Add a `moved` block from the old resource address to the new address, then confirm the replacement disappears from the plan.
Keep both resource blocks for one apply so Terraform can discover they refer to the same bucket.
Add a data source for the bucket and use it to reconcile the two resource addresses automatically.
Change the provider region so the new address is planned independently from the old state entry.

questions.explanation: State maps the real object to its Terraform address. A `moved` block records the refactor so Terraform can preserve that binding. Two resources would claim the same object, a lookup does not move state ownership, and changing provider context creates a different risk. See [Terraform moved blocks](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/block/moved).

questions.scenario: The root manages `aws_s3_bucket.orders_invoices`, but its versioning resource uses `bucket = "dp-orders-invoices-prod"`. A rename proposal updates the bucket resource but misses this literal. Which review change most directly prevents that drift?

options.text: Reference `aws_s3_bucket.orders_invoices.id` from the versioning resource.
Move the literal into a local with a different name and update it during every rename.
Add a comment beside both literals stating that reviewers must keep them equal.
Read the managed bucket through a data source and let the versioning resource depend on both declarations.

questions.explanation: A direct resource attribute reference passes the provider ID and gives Terraform dependency information. A local can reduce repeated text but still permits an independent value source, comments cannot enforce consistency, and reading an object already managed in the same root adds an unnecessary second path. See [Terraform references](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/expressions/references).
